texts.label: text
texts.orig: ABSTRACT: The permeability of poly(dimethylsiloxane) [PDMS] to H2 , O2, N2, CO2, CH4, C2H6, C3H8, CF4, C2F6, and C3 F8, and solubility of these penetrants were determined as a function of pressure at 35 °C. Permeability coefficients of perfluorinated penetrants (CF 4 , C2F6, and C3 F8) are approximately an order of magnitude lower than those of their hydrocarbon analogs (CH 4 , C2H6, and C3H8), and the perfluorocarbon permeabilities are significantly lower than even permanent gas permeability coefficients. This result is ascribed to very low perfluorocarbon solubilities in hydrocarbonbased PDMS coupled with low diffusion coefficients relative to those of their hydrocarbon analogs. The perfluorocarbons are sparingly soluble in PDMS and exhibit linear sorption isotherms. The Flory-Huggins interaction parameters for perfluorocarbon penetrants are substantially greater than those of their hydrocarbon analogs, indicating less favorable energetics of mixing perfluorocarbons with PDMS. Based on the sorption results and conventional lattice solution theory with a coordination number of 10, the formation of a single C 3 F8/PDMS segment pair requires 460 J/mol more energy than the formation of a C3H8/PDMS pair. A breakdown in the geometric mean approximation of the interaction energy between fluorocarbons and hydrocarbons was observed. These results are consistent with the solubility behavior of hydrocarbonfluorocarbon liquid mixtures and hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon gas solubility in hydrocarbon liquids. From the permeability and sorption data, diffusion coefficients were determined as a function of penetrant concentration. Perfluorocarbon diffusion coefficients are lower than those of their hydrocarbon analogs, consistent with the larger size of the fluorocarbons. © 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci B: Polym Phys 38: 415-434, 2000

texts.label: text
texts.orig: attracted attention as an environmentally important and commercially viable application of membrane technology in the semiconductor industry. 3 However, there are few reports of PFC transport properties in polymers. Stern et al. 4 reported the permeability, solubility, and diffusivity of CF 4 in polyethylene. Roberts and Ching 5 reported the permeability of polydimethylsiloxane [PDMS] composite membranes to CF4 and several Freon gases to ascertain the suitability of PDMS for Freon-permanent gas separations. They observed that PDMS was more permeable to nitrogen than to CF4. The permeability of poly(1-trimethylsilyl1-propyne) [PTMSP] to CF4 and several chlorofluorocarbons [CFCs] was reported by Pinnau and Toy 6 in their study of the organic vapor/permanent gas mixture transport properties of this glassy polymer. These investigators observed that PTMSP was less permeable to CF4 and CFCs than it was to similarly sized hydrocarbon penetrants. Recently, 7-13 fluorocarbon transport properties in several polymers have been reported by the authors. This work included a systematic study of hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon solubility in PDMS. 14 We observed very low fluorocarbon solubilities in PDMS relative to those of their hydrocarbon analogs. This behavior was ascribed to an unfavorable interaction between fluorinated penetrants and the hydrocarbon-based, liquidlike PDMS matrix.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present study extends our previous work by reporting light gas, hydrocarbon, and PFC permeability in PDMS. Our previous solubility results for these same penetrants are utilized with the permeability data to evaluate diffusion coefficients of these penetrants in PDMS. PDMS, the most permeable rubbery polymer, is used commercially in membrane-based vapor separation applications. 15 Unlike conventional glassy polymers used for permanent gas separations (e.g., O2/N2, H2/N2, etc.), PDMS is more permeable to many organic vapors, such as C3H8, than to supercritical gases, such as nitrogen. 16

texts.orig: interactions. 17 Typically, in the absence of specific interactions, the first effect is dominant and solubility increases as penetrant condensability, characterized by critical temperature, normal boiling point, or Lennard-Jones force constant, increases. 18 On this basis, solubility selectivity increases as the difference in condensability between two penetrants in a mixture increases. Diffusion coefficients decrease as penetrant size increases, and diffusivity is often correlated with measures of penetrant size, such as critical volume, van der Waals volume, Lennard-Jones diameter, or kinetic diameter. 17 Diffusivity selectivity increases as the difference in the relative size of two penetrants increases. Typically, larger penetrants are more condensable, and thus more soluble than smaller penetrants. 17 Therefore, a tradeoff exists between solubility selectivity and diffusivity selectivity, with the overall selectivity depending on the relative magnitudes of these two terms. Ordinarily, for a rubbery polymer such as PDMS, the liquidlike polymer matrix has a poor ability to sieve molecules based on size, making diffusivity a weak function of penetrant size. 17 Consequently, for a rubbery polymer, the overall selectivity is often governed by differences in penetrant solubility. 15,19

texts.orig: Poly(dimethylsiloxane) [PDMS] composite membranes were furnished by Membrane Technology and Research, Inc. (Menlo Park, CA). These membranes were prepared using poly(dimethylsiloxane) supplied by Wacker Silicones Corp. (Adrian, MI). The composite membranes were composed of a filler-free PDMS film on a highly microporous support and were utilized for pure gas permeation experiments. The 35 m m-thick PDMS separating layer was crosslinked at 100 °C using a proprietary crosslinker/catalyst system provided by Wacker Silicones Corp.

texts.orig: The permeation properties of PDMS were determined utilizing a constant pressure/variable volume apparatus. 23 The surface area of the membrane was 13.8 cm 2 . The upstream pressure was varied between 15 and 240 psig, whereas the downstream pressure was atmospheric (0 psig). Gas flow rates were measured with a soap-film bubble flowmeter. The temperature was maintained at 35 °C ( 6 0.5 °C) using a DYNA-SENSE t temperature control system. Prior to each experiment, both the upstream and downstream sides of the permeation cell were purged with pene- trant gas. The permeability coefficients of gases and vapors were determined in the following order: H 2 , O 2 , N 2 , CO2, CH4, C 2 H6, C3H8, CF4, C2F6, C3F8. texts.orig: The permeability of PDMS to H2, O2, N2, CO2, CH4, C2H6, C3H8, CF4, C2F6, and C3F8 at 35 °C as a function of the pressure difference, D p 5 p 2 2 p 1, across the polymer membrane is presented in Figure 1. The permanent gas and hydrocarbon permeability coefficients measured are very similar to those reported by Stern et al. 25 Reports of permeability coefficients of PFCs in PDMS are rare. In this regard, the permeability of PDMS to CF4 measured in this work is approximately 5 times higher than that estimated indirectly by Roberts and Ching 5 using the oxygen permeability reported by Robb 26 and their measured ratio of oxygen and CF4 fluxes in composite membranes. Our oxygen permeability coefficient is somewhat higher than that reported by Robb (800 Barrers in this work for PDMS with no filler as compared to 600 Barrers given by Robb for PDMS with 33% silica filler). However, this difference does not explain the CF4 permeability discrepancy between Roberts and Ching 5 and ourselves, the source of which is unknown.

texts.orig: examined in this study. The value of m is determined principally by the interplay between three factors: plasticization, hydrostatic pressure, and penetrant solubility. Plasticization refers to an increase in penetrant diffusivity resulting from increased polymer local segmental motion caused by the presence of penetrant molecules in the polymer matrix. 27 As penetrant pressure and, therefore, penetrant concentration in the polymer increase, the tendency to plasticize a polymer matrix increases, particularly for strongly sorbing penetrants. 27 On the other hand, high penetrant pressure acting on the polymer sample can slightly compress the polymer matrix, thereby reducing the amount of free volume available for penetrant transport and reducing penetrant diffusion coefficients. 27 In addition to these competing effects, which act on penetrant diffusion coefficients, penetrant solubility in rubbery polymers frequently increases with pressure, particularly for organic vapor penetrants, leading to a corresponding increase in permeability. 22 As a result of the interaction between these factors, the permeability coefficients of low-sorbing penetrants, such as H2, which do not plasticize PDMS and have essentially pressure independent solubility coefficients, decrease very slightly with increasing penetrant pressure (i.e., m is negative). In contrast, the permeability coefficients of more soluble penetrants, such as C3 H8, which induce significant plasticization and have solubility coefficients that also increase with pressure, increase with increasing pressure ( m is positive). The relative magnitudes of the different factors affecting the pressure dependence of permeability for a particular penetrant depend upon the solubility and diffusivity behavior of that penetrant. In subsequent sections of this work, the effect of pressure on solubility and diffusivity is discussed in greater depth.

texts.orig: cients among a group of penetrants so that more soluble penetrants are more permeable. Consequently, a penetrant's relative permeability in PDMS is largely determined by its relative solubility. This principle and the exceptions to it are demonstrated in Figure 2, which presents Po as a function of penetrant critical temperature, Tc . Critical temperature is frequently used as a scaling factor for penetrant condensability. 28 Generally, penetrants with higher critical temperatures are more soluble in polymers as well as liquids. 28 Based on the data in Figure 2, penetrant permeability increases systematically as penetrant critical temperature increases with two notable exceptions. First, the permeability coefficient of hydrogen is higher than its critical temperature suggests. This result is due to hydrogen's extremely small size, which results in diffusivity values high enough to more than compensate for low solubility. Second, PFC permeability coefficients are much lower than their critical temperatures would suggest. As discussed in more detail later in this report, this result is due to low PFC solubility coefficients (relative to what one would anticipate based on their critical temperatures) coupled with low diffusion coefficients for these large penetrants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: sure of penetrant size. Frequently, there is a strong correlation between penetrant size and transport properties in both glassy and rubbery polymers. 15 In general, as penetrant size increases, vapor permeability increases in rubbery or weakly size-sieving polymers such as PDMS and decreases in strongly size-selective glassy polymers. 15 The transport behavior of permanent gases and hydrocarbons in PDMS follows the expected trend for rubbery polymers. The larger, more condensable hydrocarbon vapors are more permeable than the light gases, and their permeability coefficients increase with increasing carbon number. In contrast, the PFC penetrants display altogether different permeability behavior. Their permeability coefficients are lower than those of the permanent gases and do not systematically increase with increasing penetrant size (carbon number). In comparison to the hydrocarbon penetrants, the PFC analogs are approximately an order of magnitude less permeable. Even among penetrants of similar size (as measured by critical volume) the PFCs are significantly less permeable. For example, CF4, which has a slightly smaller critical volume than C2H6 (139.6 cm 3 /mol for CF4 versus 148.3 cm 3 /mol for C2H6), has a permeability coefficient 16 times smaller than that of ethane. This large difference in permeabilities between similar-sized hydrocarbon and PFC penetrants suggests significant sol- ubility differences between the two families of compounds.

texts.orig: In eq 12, p is the penetrant pressure, and the slope, n , characterizes the pressure dependence of solubility. A summary of the sorption parameters for all penetrants is recorded in Table II. For the relatively low-sorbing penetrants considered (H 2 , O2, N2, CH4, CF4, C2F6, and C3F8), the pressure dependence of solubility, n , is equal to zero within the uncertainty in the sorption measurements. Thus, the solubility of these penetrants in PDMS is essentially independent of penetrant pressure and is well-described by Henry's law, which is typical of the sorption of light gases in many polymers. 25 The parameter m , which characterizes the pressure dependence of permeability, is negative for H2, O2 , N2, CF4, indicating that hydrostatic compression is the dominant factor in determining the effect of pressure on the permeability coefficients of these penetrants. For CH 4 and C2F6, in addition to solubility, permeability is also essentially independent of pressure (i.e., m ' 0), indicating no noticeable plasticization or compression effects. For C 3F8, pressure independent solubility coupled with a positive value of m suggests plasticization of PDMS by this large penetrant. The more strongly sorbing penetrants (CO2, C2H6, and C3H8) exhibit an increase in solubility with increasing penetrant pressure (i.e., n . 0). This is typical behavior for many organic vapors in rubbery polymers. 17 For these penetrants, permeability also increases with increasing pressure, indicating that either solubility ef- fects alone or solubility effects coupled with plasticization are more important than hydrostatic compression in determining the pressure dependence of permeability.

texts.orig: The unusually low solubility of the PFC penetrants in PDMS is further demonstrated in Figure 6(a,b), 14 which presents the infinite dilution solubilities, S ' , as a function of two measures of penetrant condensability, Tc and ( Tc / T ) 2 . Previous results 28 indicate that the logarithm of gas solubility in polymers often increases linearly with penetrant critical temperature. Based on the data in Figure 6(a), with the exception of the PFCs, this trend is obeyed. The solubility coefficients of the PFCs fall systematically below the expected solubility levels based on the solubility of permanent gases and hydrocarbons. The slope of the best-fit line in Figure 6(a), obtained without including the PFC data, is 0.014 6 0.001. For comparison, Van Amerongen 28 obtained a slope of 0.017 for a series of gases in natural rubber, and the data of Michaels and Bixler 34 yields a slope of 0.016 for gases in polyethylene. Using a corresponding states approach, Suwandi and Stern 35 demonstrated that the logarithm of infinite dilution solubility is a linear function of ( Tc / T ) 2 for a wide range of penetrants in PDMS. For comparison, Van Amerongen 28 obtained a slope of 0.017 for a series of gases in natural rubber, and the data of Michaels and Bixler 34 yields a slope of 0.016 for gases in polyethylene. Using a corresponding states approach, Suwandi and Stern 35 demonstrated that the logarithm of infinite dilution solubility is a linear function of ( Tc / T ) 2 for a wide range of penetrants in PDMS. A fundamental basis for this approach is provided by Bondar et al. 12 Serad et al. 38 demonstrate the validity of this correlation for several glassy polymers. In contrast, the PFC solubilities are substantially and systematically lower than those of all other penetrants considered. This result suggests an unfavorable interaction between the PFCs and the hydrocarbon-rich PDMS matrix. There is good agreement between these two sets of data. Our x # values for hydrocarbon and PFC penetrants are presented in Figure 8 as a function of penetrant critical volume. The values of the interaction parameter for the PFC penetrants are systematically higher than those of their hydrocarbon analogs, and in fact fall on a separate trendline. This result illustrates the less favorable (relative to hydrocarbons) interactions that characterize the mixing of PFCs with PDMS, and is consistent with the sorption isotherms of these penetrants.

texts.orig: Penetrant solubility in a polymer is determined by both penetrant condensability (as characterized by saturation vapor pressure, critical temperature, normal boiling point, etc.) and by polymer-penetrant interactions, as characterized by x values. 43 The interplay between these two factors determines the overall solubility of a penetrant in a polymer. For example, as shown in Table II, C3F8 solubility is nearly an order of magnitude higher than N2 solubility even though N2 has a lower (i.e., more energetically favorable) x value than C3F8 (cf. Table IV and Fig. 8). The solubility of C 3 F8 is higher than that for N 2 due to the much more condensable nature of C3F8 as indicated by its higher critical temperature and lower vapor pressure (cf. Table I).

texts.orig: In contrast, more soluble penetrants (CO2, C2H6, C3H8, and C3F8) exhibit an increase in permeability coefficients with increasing penetrant pressure. Based on the data in Table II, this increase in permeability coefficient for CO2 is a consequence of the increase in CO2 solubility in PDMS with pressure. In this regard, similar results are reported by Singh et al. 22 for acetone transport in PDMS. Ethane and propane exhibit both increasing solubility and mobility with increasing penetrant pressure. For example, the diffusion coefficient and solubility of C3H8 increase by 60% and 39%, respectively, resulting in a permeability increase of 120% over the pressure range examined. In contrast, for C3F8, the increase in permeability coefficient with increasing pressure can be ascribed entirely to increased penetrant mobility. This result suggests that the observed C3F8 pressure dependence is a result of the introduction of extra free volume that accompanies the dissolution of this large PFC penetrant in PDMS.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The source of the dramatic difference in the transport properties of the larger hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon penetrants in PDMS is clearly evident from the data in Table VII. Using C2H6 and its fluorinated analog as examples, the relatively low PFC permeability in PDMS is predominately, though not exclusively, due to low PFC solubility in PDMS. C2F6 is 6.9 times less soluble and has a diffusion coefficient 2.6 times smaller than C2H6 in PDMS. As a result, PDMS is almost 18 times more permeable to C2H6 than to C2F6. The situation is similar for C3H8 and C3F8. For methane and perfluoromethane, solubility and diffusivity differences are similar, with both factors favoring methane transport.

Polymer-penetrant interaction parameters for the PFCs are substantially greater than those of their hydrocarbon analogs, reflecting the relatively poor energetics associated with mixing PFCs with the hydrocarbon-based PDMS matrix. For a coordination number of 10, the formation of a single C3F8/PDMS segment pair requires 460 J/mol more energy than the formation of a C3H8/ PDMS segment pair. The differences in the calculated and predicted values of ( d 2 2 d 1 ) 2 are significantly larger for PFCs than for their hydrocarbon analogs. These results are consistent with the solubility behavior of hydrocarbon-fluorocarbon liquid mixtures and hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon gases in hydrocarbon liquids. The concentration averaged diffusion coefficients of the PFC penetrants are smaller than those of their hydrocarbon analogs, which is consistent with the larger size of the fluorocarbons.
